| 正式名称 = {{nihongo|儺追神事|なおいしんじ}}
| 開催時期 = Every year from the 13th day of the first lunar month (旧暦正月13日) until the following morning
| 会場 = {{ill|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q11465296|lt=Owari Taikoku Reishin Shrine|1=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine}}
| 最寄駅 = {{ill|Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|en|Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|WD=Q1137838|lt=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|1=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line}} {{ill|Kōnomiya Station|en|Kōnomiya Station|WD=Q580707|lt=Kounomiya Station|1=Kōnomiya Station}}
| URL = http://www.konomiya.or.jp/main/hadakamatsuri
}}

'''Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri''' (こうのみやはだかまつり) is the common name for the {{nihongo|Naooi Shinji|なおいしんじ}}—a Shinto ritual tradition—handed down at {{ill|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q11465296|lt=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|1=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine}} (Kounomiya) located in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City|1=Inazawa}}. In this ritual, a man designated as the {{nihongo|Shinotoko|しんおとこ}} (literally "god-man") bears calamities and defilements on his body. These are then transferred to a type of {{ill|Mochi|en|Mochi|WD=Q470519|lt=Mochi|1=Mochi}} known as {{nihongo|Dobei|どべい}}, which is subsequently buried in the ground, thereby effecting an expulsion of misfortune.<ref name="matsuri">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.inazawa.aichi.jp/miryoku/hadakafes/index.html |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri|publisher=Inazawa City|accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> This event is designated by the prefecture as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}}.

== Overview ==
[[File:Konomiya Hadaka Matsuri - 18.jpg|thumb|250px|Men gathered at the temple gate attempting to touch the god-man approaching during the Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri.]]
The individual designated as the {{nihongo|Naoi-nin|なおいにん}} (literally "one who bears the Naooi") is selected from among volunteers by drawing lots. Starting three days before the Naooi Shinji ritual, he secludes himself in the {{nihongo|Naoi-den|なおいでん}} and undertakes rigorous ascetic practices and purification ({{ill|Vīrya|en|Vīrya|WD=Q3180778|lt=shōjin|1=Vīrya}} and {{ill|saikai|ja|潔斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saikai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).

On the afternoon of the 13th day of the lunar new year, groups of men—mainly those considered to be "calamity-bearers"—gather in their traditional undergarments ({{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|WD=Q976963|lt=fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}}). They carry offerings known as {{nihongo|Naoi-gire|なおいぎれ}} (a cloth on which participants, who cannot take part in the ritual, have inscribed their names, ages, etc., as a prayer for protection) that have been fastened together with the {{nihongo|Naoi-zasa|なおいざさ}} (bamboo leaves tied together) into the haiden (worship hall) of Kounomiya Shrine, and offer them one after another<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi">{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/minzoku/mukeiminzoku/kensitei/0838.html |title=Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Bunkazai Navi Aichi|date=2019-11-25 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>{{Efn|Although the offering of the Naoi-zasa has long been performed exclusively by men—stemming from the custom of wearing fundoshi—from 2024 (Reiwa 6) women are now also permitted to participate<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/20231107/NEWS/202311070060 |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri to see first female participation: Clothed women to offer bamboo-leaf bundles before the tussle|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=November 7, 2023|edition=Morning|page=1}}</ref>}}. Soon, the shrine grounds become packed with thousands of men eagerly awaiting the arrival of the god-man. At around 3:00 PM, after the conclusion of the ritual ceremony (the festival event) of Naooi Shinji, the god-man leaps into the throng of assembled men. In the ensuing frenzied struggle—marked by shoving, wrestling, and grappling—the men attempt to touch the god-man in the belief that contact will enable them to escape misfortune<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi" />.
The climax of the festival is reached when the overwhelmed god-man, having absorbed all misfortunes, is dragged into the Naooi-den.

In the early morning at 3:00 AM the following day, after the excitement of the previous day has subsided, the priests force the god-man to carry a dobei (a lump of rice cake imbued with all impurities) on his back. They then pelt him with pebbles made from small branches fashioned from {{ill|peach|ja|桃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/peach|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モモ|1=Peach|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13189}} and {{ill|willow|ja|柳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/willow|1=柳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15884500}}, expelling him from the shrine grounds. The god-man, having been bound with all sins and defilements, discards the dobei into the dark and departs without looking back, while the priests complete the ritual of exorcising misfortune by burying the discarded dobei in the ground<ref name="navi" />. This ritual is called {{nihongo|Yonaoi Shinji|よなおいしんじ}}, and is regarded as the most important ceremony of the festival<ref name="naoi">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.inazawa.aichi.jp/miryoku/bunka/kenshitei/1002914.html |title=Prefectural Designated Cultural Property: Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Inazawa City|date=2019-01-29 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>. Moreover, the pebbles hurled at the god-man during his expulsion are reduced to ash and are incorporated into the dobei for the following year.

The related events of the festival take place over approximately half a month, from the 2nd day of the lunar new year (旧暦正月2日) to the 14th day (旧暦正月14日).
{{-}}

'''Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri''' (こうのみやはだかまつり) is the common name for the {{nihongo|Naooi Shinji|なおいしんじ}}—a Shinto ritual tradition—handed down at {{ill|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|WD=Q11465296|lt=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|1=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine}} (Kounomiya) located in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City|1=Inazawa}}. In this ritual, a man designated as the {{nihongo|Shinotoko|しんおとこ}} (literally "god-man") bears calamities and defilements on his body. These are then transferred to a type of {{ill|Mochi|en|Mochi|WD=Q470519|lt=Mochi|1=Mochi}} known as {{nihongo|Dobei|どべい}}, which is subsequently buried in the ground, thereby effecting an expulsion of misfortune.<ref name="matsuri">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.inazawa.aichi.jp/miryoku/hadakafes/index.html |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri|publisher=Inazawa City|accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> This event is designated by the prefecture as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24895900}}.

== Overview ==
[[File:Konomiya Hadaka Matsuri - 18.jpg|thumb|250px|Men gathered at the temple gate attempting to touch the god-man approaching during the Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri.]]
The individual designated as the {{nihongo|Naoi-nin|なおいにん}} (literally "one who bears the Naooi") is selected from among volunteers by drawing lots. Starting three days before the Naooi Shinji ritual, he secludes himself in the {{nihongo|Naoi-den|なおいでん}} and undertakes rigorous ascetic practices and purification ({{ill|Vīrya|en|Vīrya|WD=Q3180778|lt=shōjin|1=Vīrya}} and {{ill|saikai|ja|潔斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saikai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).

On the afternoon of the 13th day of the lunar new year, groups of men—mainly those considered to be "calamity-bearers"—gather in their traditional undergarments ({{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|WD=Q976963|lt=fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}}). They carry offerings known as {{nihongo|Naoi-gire|なおいぎれ}} (a cloth on which participants, who cannot take part in the ritual, have inscribed their names, ages, etc., as a prayer for protection) that have been fastened together with the {{nihongo|Naoi-zasa|なおいざさ}} (bamboo leaves tied together) into the haiden (worship hall) of Kounomiya Shrine, and offer them one after another<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi">{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/minzoku/mukeiminzoku/kensitei/0838.html |title=Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Bunkazai Navi Aichi|date=2019-11-25 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>{{Efn|Although the offering of the Naoi-zasa has long been performed exclusively by men—stemming from the custom of wearing fundoshi—from 2024 (Reiwa 6) women are now also permitted to participate<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/20231107/NEWS/202311070060 |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri to see first female participation: Clothed women to offer bamboo-leaf bundles before the tussle|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=November 7, 2023|edition=Morning|page=1}}</ref>}}. Soon, the shrine grounds become packed with thousands of men eagerly awaiting the arrival of the god-man. At around 3:00 PM, after the conclusion of the ritual ceremony (the festival event) of Naooi Shinji, the god-man leaps into the throng of assembled men. In the ensuing frenzied struggle—marked by shoving, wrestling, and grappling—the men attempt to touch the god-man in the belief that contact will enable them to escape misfortune<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi" />.
The climax of the festival is reached when the overwhelmed god-man, having absorbed all misfortunes, is dragged into the Naooi-den.

In the early morning at 3:00 AM the following day, after the excitement of the previous day has subsided, the priests force the god-man to carry a dobei (a lump of rice cake imbued with all impurities) on his back. They then pelt him with pebbles made from small branches fashioned from {{ill|peach|ja|桃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/peach|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モモ|1=Peach|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q13189}} and {{ill|willow|ja|柳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/willow|1=柳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q15884500}}, expelling him from the shrine grounds. Moreover, the pebbles hurled at the god-man during his expulsion are reduced to ash and are incorporated into the dobei for the following year.

The related events of the festival take place over approximately half a month, from the 2nd day of the lunar new year (旧暦正月2日) to the 14th day (旧暦正月14日).

Starting three days before the Naooi Shinji ritual, he secludes himself in the {{nihongo|Naoi-den|なおいでん}} and undertakes rigorous ascetic practices and purification ({{ill|Vīrya|en|Vīrya|qid=Q3180778|lt=shōjin|1=Vīrya}} and {{ill|saikai|ja|潔斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saikai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).

On the afternoon of the 13th day of the lunar new year, groups of men—mainly those considered to be "calamity-bearers"—gather in their traditional undergarments ({{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|qid=Q976963|lt=fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}}). They carry offerings known as {{nihongo|Naoi-gire|なおいぎれ}} (a cloth on which participants, who cannot take part in the ritual, have inscribed their names, ages, etc., as a prayer for protection) that have been fastened together with the {{nihongo|Naoi-zasa|なおいざさ}} (bamboo leaves tied together) into the haiden (worship hall) of Kounomiya Shrine, and offer them one after another<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi">{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/minzoku/mukeiminzoku/kensitei/0838.html |title=Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Bunkazai Navi Aichi|date=2019-11-25 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>{{Efn|Although the offering of the Naoi-zasa has long been performed exclusively by men—stemming from the custom of wearing fundoshi—from 2024 (Reiwa 6) women are now also permitted to participate<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/20231107/NEWS/202311070060 |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri to see first female participation: Clothed women to offer bamboo-leaf bundles before the tussle|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=November 7, 2023|edition=Morning|page=1}}</ref>}}. Soon, the shrine grounds become packed with thousands of men eagerly awaiting the arrival of the god-man. At around 3:00 PM, after the conclusion of the ritual ceremony (the festival event) of Naooi Shinji, the god-man leaps into the throng of assembled men. In the ensuing frenzied struggle—marked by shoving, wrestling, and grappling—the men attempt to touch the god-man in the belief that contact will enable them to escape misfortune<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi" />.
The climax of the festival is reached when the overwhelmed god-man, having absorbed all misfortunes, is dragged into the Naooi-den.

In the early morning at 3:00 AM the following day, after the excitement of the previous day has subsided, the priests force the god-man to carry a dobei (a lump of rice cake imbued with all impurities) on his back. They then pelt him with pebbles made from small branches fashioned from {{ill|peach|ja|桃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/peach|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モモ|1=Peach|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13189}} and {{ill|willow|ja|柳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/willow|1=柳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15884500}}, expelling him from the shrine grounds. The god-man, having been bound with all sins and defilements, discards the dobei into the dark and departs without looking back, while the priests complete the ritual of exorcising misfortune by burying the discarded dobei in the ground<ref name="navi" />. This ritual is called {{nihongo|Yonaoi Shinji|よなおいしんじ}}, and is regarded as the most important ceremony of the festival<ref name="naoi">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.inazawa.aichi.jp/miryoku/bunka/kenshitei/1002914.html |title=Prefectural Designated Cultural Property: Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Inazawa City|date=2019-01-29 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>. Moreover, the pebbles hurled at the god-man during his expulsion are reduced to ash and are incorporated into the dobei for the following year.

The related events of the festival take place over approximately half a month, from the 2nd day of the lunar new year (旧暦正月2日) to the 14th day (旧暦正月14日).
{{-}}

{{Lunisolar January}}
{{More footnotes needed|date=February 2024}}
{{イベントインフォメーション
| イベント名称 = {{ill|Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri|ja|国府宮はだか祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri|1=Konomiya Hadaka Matsuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q48743940}}
| 画像 = Kounomiya-hadakamaturi.jpg
| 画像サイズ = 280
| 種類 = {{ill|Shinto ritual|ja|神事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto ritual|1=Shinto ritual|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588671}}
| 正式名称 = {{nihongo|儺追神事|なおいしんじ}}
| 開催時期 = Every year from the 13th day of the first lunar month (旧暦正月13日) until the following morning
| 会場 = {{ill|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|qid=Q11465296|lt=Owari Taikoku Reishin Shrine|1=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine}}
| 最寄駅 = {{ill|Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|en|Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|qid=Q1137838|lt=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line|1=Meitetsu Nagoya Main Line}} {{ill|Kōnomiya Station|en|Kōnomiya Station|qid=Q580707|lt=Kounomiya Station|1=Kōnomiya Station}}
| URL = http://www.konomiya.or.jp/main/hadakamatsuri
}}

'''Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri''' (こうのみやはだかまつり) is the common name for the {{nihongo|Naooi Shinji|なおいしんじ}}—a Shinto ritual tradition—handed down at {{ill|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|en|Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|qid=Q11465296|lt=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine|1=Owari Ōkunitama Shrine}} (Kounomiya) located in {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|qid=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}, {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|qid=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City|1=Inazawa}}. In this ritual, a man designated as the {{nihongo|Shinotoko|しんおとこ}} (literally "god-man") bears calamities and defilements on his body. These are then transferred to a type of {{ill|Mochi|en|Mochi|qid=Q470519|lt=Mochi|1=Mochi}} known as {{nihongo|Dobei|どべい}}, which is subsequently buried in the ground, thereby effecting an expulsion of misfortune.<ref name="matsuri">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.inazawa.aichi.jp/miryoku/hadakafes/index.html |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri|publisher=Inazawa City|accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> This event is designated by the prefecture as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24895900}}.

== Overview ==
[[File:Konomiya Hadaka Matsuri - 18.jpg|thumb|250px|Men gathered at the temple gate attempting to touch the god-man approaching during the Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri.]]
The individual designated as the {{nihongo|Naoi-nin|なおいにん}} (literally "one who bears the Naooi") is selected from among volunteers by drawing lots. Starting three days before the Naooi Shinji ritual, he secludes himself in the {{nihongo|Naoi-den|なおいでん}} and undertakes rigorous ascetic practices and purification ({{ill|Vīrya|en|Vīrya|qid=Q3180778|lt=shōjin|1=Vīrya}} and {{ill|saikai|ja|潔斎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saikai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).

On the afternoon of the 13th day of the lunar new year, groups of men—mainly those considered to be "calamity-bearers"—gather in their traditional undergarments ({{ill|Fundoshi|en|Fundoshi|qid=Q976963|lt=fundoshi|1=Fundoshi}}). They carry offerings known as {{nihongo|Naoi-gire|なおいぎれ}} (a cloth on which participants, who cannot take part in the ritual, have inscribed their names, ages, etc., as a prayer for protection) that have been fastened together with the {{nihongo|Naoi-zasa|なおいざさ}} (bamboo leaves tied together) into the haiden (worship hall) of Kounomiya Shrine, and offer them one after another<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi">{{Cite web|url=https://www.pref.aichi.jp/kyoiku/bunka/bunkazainavi/minzoku/mukeiminzoku/kensitei/0838.html |title=Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Bunkazai Navi Aichi|date=2019-11-25 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>{{Efn|Although the offering of the Naoi-zasa has long been performed exclusively by men—stemming from the custom of wearing fundoshi—from 2024 (Reiwa 6) women are now also permitted to participate<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.chunichi.co.jp/article/20231107/NEWS/202311070060 |title=Kounomiya Hadaka Matsuri to see first female participation: Clothed women to offer bamboo-leaf bundles before the tussle|newspaper=Chunichi Shimbun|date=November 7, 2023|edition=Morning|page=1}}</ref>}}. Soon, the shrine grounds become packed with thousands of men eagerly awaiting the arrival of the god-man. At around 3:00 PM, after the conclusion of the ritual ceremony (the festival event) of Naooi Shinji, the god-man leaps into the throng of assembled men. In the ensuing frenzied struggle—marked by shoving, wrestling, and grappling—the men attempt to touch the god-man in the belief that contact will enable them to escape misfortune<ref name="matsuri" /><ref name="navi" />.
The climax of the festival is reached when the overwhelmed god-man, having absorbed all misfortunes, is dragged into the Naooi-den.

In the early morning at 3:00 AM the following day, after the excitement of the previous day has subsided, the priests force the god-man to carry a dobei (a lump of rice cake imbued with all impurities) on his back. They then pelt him with pebbles made from small branches fashioned from {{ill|peach|ja|桃|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/peach|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to モモ|1=Peach|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q13189}} and {{ill|willow|ja|柳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/willow|1=柳|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15884500}}, expelling him from the shrine grounds. The god-man, having been bound with all sins and defilements, discards the dobei into the dark and departs without looking back, while the priests complete the ritual of exorcising misfortune by burying the discarded dobei in the ground<ref name="navi" />. This ritual is called {{nihongo|Yonaoi Shinji|よなおいしんじ}}, and is regarded as the most important ceremony of the festival<ref name="naoi">{{Cite web|url=http://www.city.inazawa.aichi.jp/miryoku/bunka/kenshitei/1002914.html |title=Prefectural Designated Cultural Property: Kounomiya's Naooi Festival|publisher=Inazawa City|date=2019-01-29 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref>. Moreover, the pebbles hurled at the god-man during his expulsion are reduced to ash and are incorporated into the dobei for the following year.

The related events of the festival take place over approximately half a month, from the 2nd day of the lunar new year (旧暦正月2日) to the 14th day (旧暦正月14日).
